claim: Token aids (large payloads of crafted tokens disguised as harmless content) can be used to probe AI systems and potentially reveal which model is running under the hood
actionable: When securing AI systems, implement token-based filtering and rate limiting to prevent attackers from using token aids and other probe attacks to discover model information

claim: Siege attacks (flooding AI agents with multiple large token payloads simultaneously) can drain API token budgets and payment limits, representing an economic attack vector
actionable: Implement rate limiting, token budget caps, and request throttling in AI systems to prevent economic denial-of-service attacks where attackers exhaust quota by sending spam payloads

claim: Prompt injection attempts can be filtered by robust models (Opus 4.6) but are more likely to succeed against smaller or older models, making model choice critical for security
actionable: Use the best available frontier models (Claude Opus, GPT-4) as first-line defense in production systems; smaller models will fall for prompt injection attacks more easily

claim: Structured jailbreak templates disguised as system commands or internal instructions are common attack patterns that attempt to trigger format overrides or prompt injection
actionable: Train security systems to detect and block structured jailbreak patterns including fake system commands, thinking tags used deceptively, and format override attempts

claim: No AI system is permanently secure; security requires multiple layers including email filtering, content scanning, model choice, and continuous monitoring and updates
actionable: Adopt defense-in-depth: use spam filters, implement content validation, choose strong models, add quarantine logic, enable human monitoring, and plan for regular security reviews
